title: Ensaimada
subtitle: Mallorcan coiled pastry with lard-laminated layers and icing sugar
excerpt: Ensaimada is a rich, slightly sweet coiled pastry from Mallorca, made by stretching an enriched dough very thin, spreading it with lard, then rolling and coiling it. The coil proofs for a long time before baking, developing a soft, slightly layered interior and a pale, tender crust. It is finished with a heavy dusting of icing sugar. The total time, including the long proof, is about 4 hours.
sourceNotes: Ensaimada has been made in Mallorca since at least the seventeenth century. The name comes from 'saim', the Mallorcan word for lard. It is documented as a distinct pastry in Majorcan cookbooks from the nineteenth century and became a protected designation product (Indicacio Geografica Protegida) in 2003. Jose Pizarro includes a standard recipe in 'Jose Pizarro's Spanish Seasonal Food' (2012). The three-stage enriched method here follows the Mallorcan home-baking tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shaped ensaimadas can proof overnight at room temperature, covered loosely. Bake the following morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: The shaping technique that gives ensaimada its characteristic spiral form. The rested dough is stretched very thin, spread with softened lard, then rolled into a long, thin cylinder and coiled into a flat spiral on the baking tray. The coil is left with space between the turns, as the dough will expand outward during the long proof and fill the gaps. After baking the turns will have merged into a continuous spiral. The key is stretching the dough thin enough before rolling: aim for about 2 mm, which produces the soft, layered interior.

content.text: The long, slow proof is what makes an ensaimada worth making: the dough relaxes and develops a flavour and texture that a short proof cannot replicate.
the lard-spread dough creates the layers; spacing the coil's turns generously on the tray gives the dough room to expand outward during the proof.

Lard is traditional and produces a richer, more characteristically Mallorcan result than butter. Softened unsalted butter is an acceptable substitute and is how many mainland Spanish bakeries now make it. The difference is subtle in a home bake.

Combine the 300 g flour, 5 g yeast, 5 g salt and 50 g caster sugar. Add the 100 ml tepid milk and 2 beaten eggs and mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Place in an oiled bowl, cover and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes until risen by about half.

Divide the dough into 4 pieces. Oil the work surface generously. Stretch one piece out to a very thin rectangle, about 30 x 20 cm and no more than 2 mm thick. Spread a quarter of the 70 g softened lard over the surface in a thin, even layer, leaving a 1 cm border. Roll the dough tightly from one long edge into a thin cylinder, then coil it into a flat spiral on a lined baking tray, leaving gaps between the turns. Repeat with the remaining pieces.

Cover loosely and leave to proof for 2 hours until the coils have puffed and the gaps between the turns have filled in. Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Bake for 16 to 20 minutes until the tops are pale golden brown and the pastry sounds hollow when tapped on the base. Cool on a rack, then dust heavily with icing sugar before serving.

items.problem: Layers did not develop and the interior is bread-like rather than pastry-like
items.solution: The dough was not stretched thin enough before spreading the lard, or the lard was added too thickly. The dough should be almost translucent when stretched correctly.
The gluten needs more relaxation time. After the bulk ferment, let the divided pieces rest for 10 minutes covered before stretching. Return to rest if they resist while you are working.

items.problem: Ensaimada browned too quickly before the inside was cooked
items.solution: The oven was too hot, or the dough did not proof long enough and the inside was still dense. Reduce to 180 degrees C and give the proof the full 2 hours. Cover loosely with foil if the top is colouring at 10 minutes.
